title: Starter relay contact corrosion — clicking but no crank across all makes
description: The starter relay (also called starter solenoid on Harleys) is an electromagnetic switch that routes high-current battery power to the starter motor when the start button is pressed. The relay's internal copper contacts pit and corrode over time from the high-current arcing that occurs every time they close. Once the contacts develop enough resistance, the relay clicks audibly (the electromagnetic coil still works) but can't pass enough current to spin the starter motor. This is the single most common no-crank condition on every motorcycle brand — Honda, Yamaha, Kawasaki, Suzuki, and Harley-Davidson all use the same basic relay design. On Japanese bikes the relay is typically mounted under the seat or behind a side cover near the battery. On Harleys it's usually bolted to the frame near the battery or under the electrical cover on Sportsters. The failure mode is progressive: first the starter cranks slowly on hot days (higher resistance when warm), then it becomes intermittent (sometimes clicks, sometimes cranks), then it fails completely (click only, no crank).
symptoms: clicking noise when pressing start button
causes: Internal copper contacts pitted and oxidized from repeated high-current arcing
Relay coil ground wire corroded — insufficient magnetic force to fully close contacts
Relay mounting bolt loose — poor ground path through frame
Water intrusion into relay housing from pressure washing or rain exposure
Undersized relay on older bikes — original equipment contacts rated too low for repeated use
fix_procedure: 1. Locate the starter relay — follow the large-gauge red wire from the battery positive terminal. On Honda CBR/CB models it's typically under the rear seat on the left side. On Kawasaki ZX/Ninja models it's behind the right side cover. On Yamaha R-series it's under the seat near the battery box. On Harleys it's on the frame near the battery or inside the electrical cover (Sportster). 2. Diagnostic test: with the key ON and kill switch in RUN, press the start button and listen. A single click = relay coil energizes but contacts won't pass current. No click at all = relay coil isn't getting signal (different problem — check safety switches). Rapid clicking = battery too weak to hold the relay closed. 3. Voltage drop test across relay: put multimeter on DC volts, touch one probe to the battery-side large terminal, the other to the starter-motor-side large terminal. Press the start button. Reading should be less than 0.3V. If it reads 2V+ the contacts have excessive resistance. 4. Bypass test (DIAGNOSTIC ONLY — not a permanent fix): bridge the two large terminals on the relay with a heavy screwdriver or jumper cable while the bike is in neutral. If the starter spins, the relay is confirmed bad. CAUTION: this bypasses all safety switches — the bike will crank regardless of clutch, kickstand, or kill switch position. Never do this with the bike in gear. 5. Replacement: OEM relays are $25-50 depending on make. Universal relays from automotive suppliers work but verify the coil voltage (12V), contact current rating (at least 100A), and terminal configuration. 6. Cleaning (temporary fix): some relays can be opened by prying the crimped cover. Sand the contacts with 600-grit sandpaper, clean with contact cleaner, reassemble. This buys 3-6 months but the contacts are now thinner and will fail again faster. 7. Forum tip: the starter relay is a $25-40 part that strands more motorcyclists than any other single component. Carry a spare in your tool kit on any bike over 10 years old — it takes 5 minutes to swap and can save a tow. The bypass test (bridging the large terminals with a screwdriver) is the roadside trick every rider should know. If the starter spins when you bridge the relay, you know the relay is bad and the starter motor is fine. On Harleys, the starter relay is often called the 'starter solenoid' and is a larger, more robust unit than Japanese bike relays — but it still fails the same way. Harley solenoids run $30-60 OEM. On Honda CBR models, the relay is the same part number across the 600RR, 1000RR, CB650, and many other models — OEM part 38501-GE2-018 or equivalent. Suzuki uses a nearly identical relay across the GSX-R600, GSX-R750, GSX-R1000, SV650, and V-Strom families. The aftermarket 'universal' starter relays on Amazon for $8-12 work but have thinner contact material and shorter lifespan — OEM is worth the extra $15-20. If you're replacing the relay, also clean the large terminal connections with a wire brush and apply dielectric grease — corroded terminals cause the same high-resistance symptoms as bad relay contacts.
parts_needed: Starter relay/solenoid (OEM preferred — Honda 38501-GE2-018, Suzuki 31800-47D00, or equivalent)
Dielectric grease (for terminal protection after install)
Wire brush or sandpaper 600-grit (for terminal cleaning)
Multimeter (for voltage drop test)
Spare relay for tool kit (on bikes over 10 years old)

title: Starter motor brush wear and commutator degradation — slow or no crank
description: Motorcycle starter motors are brushed DC motors with carbon brushes that conduct current to the spinning armature through a segmented copper commutator. The brushes wear down over thousands of start cycles until they're too short to maintain spring pressure against the commutator. Simultaneously, the commutator segments develop carbon buildup, pitting, and glazing between the segments. A worn starter motor cranks progressively slower over weeks/months, then one day won't crank at all. This is especially common on high-mileage touring bikes (Honda Gold Wing, Harley-Davidson touring models, Kawasaki Concours) that see 50,000+ miles and thousands of start cycles. The starter motor itself almost never 'burns out' suddenly — it's always a gradual wear process that riders ignore until complete failure.
causes: Carbon brushes worn below minimum length — insufficient spring pressure against commutator
Commutator segments glazed with carbon — poor electrical contact between brush and commutator
Commutator segment insulation raised — mica between segments protrudes above copper surface
Armature shaft bearing worn — armature wobbles, causing uneven brush wear and eventual contact loss
Brush springs fatigued — reduced pressure even with adequate brush length remaining
fix_procedure: 1. Remove starter motor from engine. On most Japanese bikes: 2 bolts on the front of the engine (behind the radiator on faired bikes). On Harleys: 2 bolts on the primary side (Twin Cam) or inner primary (Evo/Sportster). 2. Disassemble: remove through-bolts (2 or 3 long bolts holding the end caps together), separate end caps from motor housing. 3. Inspect brushes: measure length against specification (typically 12-14mm new, replace at 5-7mm). Brushes should move freely in their holders — carbon dust can bind them. 4. Inspect commutator: surface should be smooth, polished copper-colored. If glazed (shiny, dark), lightly sand with 400-grit sandpaper while spinning the armature by hand. If segments are pitted or grooved, the commutator can be turned on a lathe (machine shop, $30-50). 5. Undercut mica: the insulation between commutator segments should be 0.5-0.8mm below the copper surface. If mica is level with or above the copper, undercut it with a small hacksaw blade or dedicated mica undercutter. 6. Check armature continuity: using a multimeter on the lowest ohms scale, check resistance between adjacent commutator segments — they should all read the same low resistance. An open circuit between any two adjacent segments means a burned winding — replace the armature or the whole starter. 7. Reassemble with new brushes, ensuring brush leads aren't pinched and springs seat correctly. 8. Bench test: apply 12V directly to the starter (clamp it securely — it has significant torque). It should spin freely and smoothly with no grinding or wobbling. 9. Forum tip: a starter motor rebuild kit (brushes + O-rings + sometimes bearings) costs $25-50 and takes 2-3 hours — versus $150-400 for a new/rebuilt starter motor. On Harley-Davidson models, the 1.2kW and 1.4kW starters used on Twin Cam and M8 engines are rebuildable with readily available brush kits from Drag Specialties or Tucker Rocky ($35-45). The commutator cleaning alone often restores a 'dead' starter — the carbon glaze acts as an insulator. On Honda Gold Wings (GL1500, GL1800), the starter motor is buried and takes 2-3 hours just to access — which is why shops quote $400-600 for starter service. If you're in there, replace the brushes proactively even if they look okay. On high-compression engines (Harley Twin Cam 103/110, Honda VTX1800), the starter works harder and wears brushes faster — check every 30,000 miles. A rebuild is almost always more cost-effective than replacement: $40 in parts + your labor versus $250+ for a new unit. The only time to replace outright is if the armature windings are burned (you'll see discoloration and smell burnt insulation) or the commutator is too deeply grooved to turn.
parts_needed: Starter motor brush kit (4 brushes + springs, make/model specific)
Commutator end bearing (replace proactively while apart)
O-ring kit for starter motor end caps
400-grit sandpaper (for commutator cleaning)
Mica undercutting tool or small hacksaw blade
Dielectric grease (for reassembly)

title: Starter clutch (sprag/one-way bearing) failure — starter spins but engine doesn't crank
description: The starter clutch is a one-way bearing mechanism (sprag clutch or roller clutch) that transmits rotation from the starter motor to the crankshaft in one direction only — it locks when the starter drives the engine and freewheels when the engine is running faster than the starter. When the sprag rollers or sprags wear, they slip instead of locking, and the starter motor spins freely without turning the engine over. The classic symptom is the starter motor whirring at high speed with no engine cranking — it sounds like it's spinning in free air. This is a well-known failure mode on Honda (CX500, VFR800, CBR900RR, CB750 DOHC), Kawasaki (ZX-6R, ZX-10R, ZX-14R), and Suzuki (GSX-R series, Hayabusa). The failure is more common on high-compression engines because the starter clutch experiences higher torque loads during each start cycle.
symptoms: starter spins freely without engaging engine
whirring noise when pressing start button
causes: Sprag rollers/sprags worn — can't lock against outer race under load
One-way bearing cage broken — rollers displaced from proper orientation
Starter clutch bolt(s) loosened from vibration — clutch assembly slips on crankshaft gear
Insufficient oil reaching starter clutch — bearing surfaces scored from running dry
Extreme cold thickening oil — starter clutch slips during cold starts in winter
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnostic confirmation: when you press the start button, the starter motor spins (you can hear it whirring) but the engine doesn't turn over at all — the crankshaft isn't moving. This is distinctly different from a weak battery (slow cranking) or bad relay (click only). 2. Access varies by model. On most inline-4 engines: remove the left engine cover (stator side) to access the starter clutch behind the flywheel/rotor. On some V-twin and V4 engines the starter clutch is behind the clutch basket on the right side. 3. Remove the flywheel/rotor nut (usually requires an impact gun — these are torqued to 80-120 ft-lbs and Loctited). Use a flywheel puller (model-specific or universal with correct thread) to remove the rotor. 4. Behind the rotor: the starter clutch assembly. Remove it and inspect: the rollers/sprags should lock firmly in one direction and freewheel smoothly in the other. If they slip in both directions or feel rough, the unit is worn. 5. Replace as a complete assembly — individual rollers are not typically available, and if one is worn they're all worn. 6. Torque the rotor nut to specification with fresh medium-strength Loctite (blue). 7. Forum tip: starter clutch failure is one of the most dreaded repairs on Japanese sport bikes because it requires pulling the flywheel/stator assembly — a 4-6 hour job if you've never done it, 2-3 hours if you have. The parts themselves are $80-200 OEM depending on make and model. On Kawasaki ZX-10R and ZX-14R models, the starter clutch is a known weak point — Kawasaki revised the part multiple times. Always use the latest superseded part number. On Honda VFR800 (all years), the starter clutch is behind the right-side clutch cover and is actually easier to access than most — budget 2 hours. On Suzuki GSX-R models (600/750/1000), the starter clutch is the Torx-bolted assembly behind the rotor — the bolts require blue Loctite on reassembly or they'll back out. The Hayabusa (GSX1300R) starter clutch handles enormous compression loads and fails around 40,000-60,000 miles. A heavy-duty aftermarket unit from APE ($180-250) outlasts OEM by 2-3x on any high-compression engine. The key diagnostic differentiator: if the starter motor sounds like it's spinning fast and free (high-pitched whir) rather than the normal slow, labored cranking sound — that's a starter clutch, not a starter motor or battery problem.
parts_needed: Starter clutch assembly (OEM or APE heavy-duty, model-specific)
Flywheel/rotor puller (model-specific thread)
Flywheel nut (replace — often deformed during removal)
Medium-strength threadlocker (Loctite Blue 242)
Stator cover gasket (replace whenever the cover is removed)
Impact gun (for flywheel nut removal — hand tools usually can't break the torque + Loctite)

title: Clutch safety switch failure — bike won't start even with clutch pulled in
description: Most motorcycles manufactured after the mid-1990s include a clutch safety switch that prevents the starter from engaging unless the clutch lever is fully pulled in. The switch is mounted on the clutch lever perch and is a simple normally-open contact that closes when the lever compresses it. These switches fail from water intrusion, corrosion of the internal contacts, physical damage from a drop, or the switch body cracking from UV exposure. When the switch fails open, the ECU/starter circuit doesn't receive the 'clutch is in' signal, so it refuses to energize the starter relay — the bike appears completely dead even though battery, relay, and starter are all fine. This is one of the most frustrating no-start conditions because everything else checks out. The switch design is nearly identical across Honda, Yamaha, Kawasaki, Suzuki, and Harley-Davidson — a two-wire microswitch triggered by lever travel.
symptoms: no response when pressing start button
starter works in neutral but not in gear
causes: Internal contact corrosion from water intrusion into switch housing
Switch plunger stuck in open position from dirt and debris
Wire connector at switch corroded or disconnected from vibration
Switch body cracked from UV degradation or crash damage
Clutch lever free play too large — lever doesn't fully depress the switch plunger even when pulled to bar
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnostic: put the bike in neutral. If it starts in neutral but refuses to crank when in gear with the clutch pulled in — the clutch switch is the prime suspect. Some ECUs also check the clutch switch even in neutral on newer models. 2. Locate the switch: it's on the clutch lever perch (left handlebar), usually with a 2-pin connector running down to the harness. On Harleys it's integrated into the handlebar switch housing on some models. 3. Quick test: unplug the 2-pin connector and bridge the two wires with a small jumper wire or paperclip. If the bike now starts, the switch is confirmed bad. 4. Measure switch with multimeter: set to continuity/ohms. With clutch lever released: should read open (infinite resistance). With clutch lever fully pulled: should read closed (near zero ohms). Any deviation = bad switch. 5. Replacement: $15-30 OEM for most Japanese bikes. Remove the two small screws holding it to the lever perch, unplug the connector, reverse for install. Adjust the switch position so it closes with about 10mm of lever travel from the fully-pulled position. 6. Bypass (permanent or temporary): bridge the two wires at the connector and tape/heat-shrink the connection. This permanently eliminates the clutch interlock — the bike will crank in any gear without the clutch. SAFETY WARNING: this means the bike can lurch forward if started in gear with the clutch out. Many track riders bypass this switch intentionally. 7. Forum tip: the clutch safety switch is a $15-25 part that causes more 'my bike is completely dead and I've replaced the battery, relay, and starter' posts on forums than any other component. The diagnostic shortcut: unplug the switch connector and bridge the wires. If the bike starts, order a new switch and move on — don't waste hours checking other components. On Suzuki SV650 and GSX-R models, the clutch switch is a notorious failure point, especially on bikes ridden in rain. The OEM Suzuki switch (part 37560-33E00 or equivalent) is $18 and a 10-minute install. On Honda CBR models, the switch connector is behind the left fairing stay — access requires removing the left mid-fairing. On Kawasaki Ninja models, the switch connector is usually accessible without any fairing removal. On Harley-Davidson models with integrated handlebar switches (2014+ Touring, 2018+ Softail), the clutch switch is internal to the switch housing and requires disassembly of the housing — budget 30 minutes versus 10 minutes on Japanese bikes. The bypass is the most popular 'mod' on track bikes across all makes — one jumper wire eliminates a failure point. For street bikes, keep the switch functional for safety but carry a small jumper wire in your tool kit for roadside emergencies.
parts_needed: Clutch safety switch (OEM — Suzuki 37560-33E00, Honda 35330-KYJ-901, or equivalent)
2-pin connector (if original is corroded beyond cleaning)
Dielectric grease (weatherproofing after install)
Small jumper wire (for roadside emergency bypass)
Multimeter (for switch continuity test)

title: Kickstand safety switch corrosion — no-start from weather exposure
description: The side stand (kickstand) safety switch prevents the engine from starting or running when the kickstand is down and the transmission is in gear. The switch is mounted at the base of the kickstand pivot and is exposed to road spray, rain, mud, salt, and debris — the worst possible environment for an electrical switch. The switch is typically a plunger-type contact that closes when the kickstand is fully retracted. Corrosion of the switch contacts, damage to the wiring from road debris, or physical damage from the kickstand catching on obstacles are the primary failure modes. When the switch fails open, the bike either won't start at all or stalls immediately when put into gear. This is universal across all makes: Honda, Yamaha, Kawasaki, Suzuki, and Harley-Davidson all use nearly identical designs mounted in an identically hostile location.
symptoms: engine stalls when shifting into gear
intermittent no-start after riding in rain
causes: Switch contacts corroded from road spray, rain, and salt exposure
Switch plunger stuck from mud and debris packed into the pivot area
Wiring harness abraded or cut by road debris near the kickstand mount
Switch connector corroded — resistance too high for ECU to read as closed
Kickstand spring weak — stand doesn't fully retract to depress switch plunger
Physical impact damage from kickstand catching on speed bumps or trail obstacles
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnostic: if the bike starts in neutral with kickstand down, but stalls immediately when you click into first gear — the kickstand switch is working correctly but you forgot to retract the stand. If it won't start AT ALL regardless of kickstand position or gear selection — the switch has failed open and the ECU thinks the stand is always down. 2. Locate the switch: follow the kickstand pivot bolt up to the frame bracket. The switch is a small plastic-bodied unit with a plunger or roller that contacts the kickstand arm. A 2- or 3-wire connector runs up to the main harness (usually routed along the frame tube). 3. Clean first: remove the switch, clean the plunger and housing with contact cleaner, verify the plunger moves freely. Clean the connector pins with contact cleaner and a small pick. Reinstall and test. 4. Measure with multimeter: with kickstand up (plunger depressed): should read closed (near zero ohms). With kickstand down (plunger extended): should read open. If the switch doesn't change state when you operate the plunger — replace it. 5. Replacement: $20-45 OEM depending on make. Involves removing the kickstand pivot bolt on some models — support the bike securely first. 6. Bypass: bridge the appropriate wires in the connector to permanently tell the ECU the kickstand is up. On most bikes this is the 2 outer wires of a 3-wire connector (the middle wire is typically ground). VERIFY YOUR SPECIFIC MODEL'S WIRING before bridging — wrong wires can blow a fuse or damage the ECU. SAFETY WARNING: bypassing this switch means the engine will NOT stall when you ride away with the kickstand down — this has caused crashes. 7. Preventive maintenance: apply dielectric grease to the switch body, plunger, and connector every 6 months or after any ride in heavy rain. Clean the kickstand pivot area of mud and debris regularly. 8. Forum tip: the kickstand switch is the most weather-abused electrical component on any motorcycle because of where it's mounted — 12 inches off the ground, directly in the road spray path. On bikes ridden year-round or in wet climates (Pacific Northwest, UK, northern Europe), this switch fails every 3-5 years. The repair is easy: $20-45 for a new switch, 20 minutes to install. The aggravation is high: you're stranded at a gas station in the rain because a $25 switch corroded. Preventive dielectric grease application is the solution — spend 5 minutes twice a year and the switch lasts the life of the bike. On Yamaha R6 and R1 models, the kickstand switch wiring runs along the left frame downtube where it gets hit by front tire spray — inspect the wiring for abrasion annually. On Kawasaki models, the switch is mounted higher on the pivot bracket and fails less often than Honda or Yamaha designs. On Harley-Davidson models (all Big Twins), the jiffy stand switch is a robust unit but the connector at the frame is exposed and corrodes — the connector fails more often than the switch itself. A dab of dielectric grease in the connector every oil change prevents this entirely. The roadside fix on any bike: identify and bridge the switch wires at the connector. Every model-specific forum has a thread titled 'kickstand switch bypass' with the exact wire colors to bridge.
parts_needed: Kickstand safety switch (OEM — Yamaha 5EB-82566-00, Honda 35700-MFL-003, or equivalent)
Dielectric grease (critical for weatherproofing — apply at install and every 6 months)
Contact cleaner (for switch and connector cleaning)
Wire brush (for connector pin cleaning)
Heat shrink tubing (if bypass is chosen)
Multimeter (for switch continuity test)

title: Kill switch and handlebar control switch failure — no power to ignition or starter
description: The right handlebar switch housing contains the kill switch (engine stop switch) and start button, along with the headlight and throttle housing on some models. The kill switch is a simple rocker or slider that interrupts either the ignition circuit (older bikes) or sends a signal to the ECU (newer bikes). The start button is a momentary contact switch that energizes the starter relay circuit. Both switches live in a hostile environment: exposed to weather, vibration, and the rider's gloves. Internal contacts corrode, the switch detent wears allowing the kill switch to sit in a halfway position, and the start button contacts develop enough resistance that they can't pass the starter relay coil current. A failed kill switch in the OFF position prevents all ignition — no spark, no fuel pump, no start. A failed start button prevents starter engagement even though all other systems are live. These are common failures on bikes over 10 years old across all makes.
symptoms: no response when pressing start button
causes: Kill switch internal contacts corroded — switch appears to be in RUN but contacts aren't making connection
Kill switch detent worn — switch sits between RUN and OFF positions
Start button contacts oxidized — insufficient current flow to energize starter relay coil
Water intrusion into handlebar switch housing from rain or washing
Wire harness connector at the switch housing corroded or loose from vibration
Physical damage from a crash — switch housing cracked, internal mechanism displaced
fix_procedure: 1. First check: flip the kill switch to RUN, verify it clicks firmly into position. If the detent feels mushy or the switch rocks between positions, the detent is worn. 2. Kill switch diagnostic: with key ON and kill switch in RUN, check for 12V at the ignition coil positive terminal (or fuel pump relay on FI bikes). If no voltage, the kill switch isn't passing power. Flip the switch rapidly several times — sometimes corroded contacts will make intermittent connection. 3. Start button diagnostic: with key ON, kill switch in RUN, neutral selected — put multimeter on DC volts across the starter relay coil terminals. Press start button: you should see battery voltage at the relay coil. If no voltage at the relay coil when the button is pressed, the start button contacts are bad. 4. Disassemble switch housing: remove the two screws holding the housing halves together (Phillips or Allen depending on make). Carefully separate the halves — small springs and detent balls will try to escape. 5. Clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner and fine sandpaper (800-grit). Apply a thin film of dielectric grease to the contacts. 6. Inspect the wiring and connector at the switch housing — look for green corrosion on the pins. Clean with contact cleaner and a pick. 7. Reassemble with all springs and detent balls in position. If the kill switch detent doesn't click firmly — replace the switch assembly. 8. Forum tip: the kill switch is the most overlooked cause of a no-start condition because riders assume 'it's in the RUN position, I can see it.' But the contacts can be corroded internally even when the switch appears correct externally. The diagnostic shortcut: with the key on, does the fuel pump prime (FI bikes) or do the dash lights come on? If not — flip the kill switch off and on several times rapidly. If the dash lights flicker, the kill switch is corroded. On Honda CB and CBR models, the right switch housing is a single assembly — the kill switch, start button, and headlight switch are all one unit. OEM replacement is $80-150 depending on model. Aftermarket housings from eBay ($25-40) work but have cheaper plastic and looser switch feel. On Kawasaki Ninja and Z models, the switch housings use a separate connector for each function — you can isolate exactly which switch is bad by checking each connector independently. On Harley-Davidson models, the right switch housing is model-specific and often includes the throttle-by-wire sensor on 2008+ touring models — replacement is $150-250 OEM and requires throttle position sensor relearn on some models. On Yamaha R-series and MT models, the switch connector is a single multi-pin plug behind the headlight bucket — access requires removing the front fairing or headlight. For ANY bike with intermittent no-start: the kill switch and start button should be the first two things checked after the battery. Carry a can of CRC Contact Cleaner ($5) in your garage — a 30-second spray into the switch housing every year prevents most failures.
parts_needed: Right handlebar switch housing assembly (OEM preferred — Honda 35130-MFJ-D01, or equivalent)
Electrical contact cleaner (CRC or similar)
800-grit sandpaper (for contact cleaning)
Dielectric grease (for contact protection after cleaning)
Small Phillips and Allen key set (for housing disassembly)
Replacement detent springs and balls (if lost during disassembly — often not sold separately, requiring full housing replacement)

title: Battery cable voltage drop — terminal corrosion causing slow crank or no crank
description: The battery cables are the highest-current conductors on a motorcycle, carrying 80-200+ amps during cranking. Corrosion at the battery terminals, frame ground point, starter motor connection, or any inline connector creates resistance that robs voltage from the starter motor. A starter motor that needs 10V to crank effectively will crank slowly at 9V and not at all below 8V — and 1-2V of loss across corroded connections is common on bikes over 5 years old. The insidious aspect: a voltmeter at the battery shows 12.6V with the key off, suggesting a good battery. But under cranking load, the voltage at the STARTER drops to 8-9V because 2-3V is being lost across corroded connections between the battery and the starter. This is the most commonly misdiagnosed starting problem across all makes — riders replace the battery, then the relay, then the starter motor, never realizing the $0 fix was cleaning the cable connections.
symptoms: clicking noise when pressing start button
causes: Battery terminal corrosion (white/green/blue powder) creating high-resistance connection
Frame ground cable connection corroded — ground path resistance too high
Positive cable connection at starter relay corroded or loose
Starter motor cable terminal corroded where it attaches to the starter
Aftermarket battery with slightly different terminal post size — poor clamp contact
Battery hold-down missing or loose — vibration loosens terminal connections over time
fix_procedure: 1. Visual inspection: look for any white, green, or blue corrosion on the battery terminals, cable clamps, frame ground bolt, starter relay terminals, and starter motor terminal. ANY visible corrosion = resistance = voltage loss. 2. Voltage drop test (the definitive diagnostic for cable/connection problems): a. Set multimeter to DC volts, 20V scale. b. Connect RED probe to battery POSITIVE terminal. Connect BLACK probe to the starter motor POSITIVE terminal (the large bolt on the starter). c. Have someone press the start button. d. Read the voltage while cranking. This number should be LESS THAN 0.5V. If it reads 1V+, there is excessive resistance in the positive circuit between the battery and the starter. e. Repeat for the ground side: RED probe on starter motor case (ground), BLACK probe on battery NEGATIVE terminal. Same test — should be less than 0.5V while cranking. f. If either side reads high, test individual connections to isolate: battery terminal to cable, cable to relay, relay to starter. 3. Cleaning: disconnect battery (NEGATIVE first, always). Remove cable clamps. Clean battery posts with a battery terminal brush ($5, purpose-built tool). Clean cable clamp interiors with the brush's other end. Clean the frame ground bolt and mounting surface to bare metal with a wire brush. Clean all large-terminal connections at relay and starter motor. 4. Reassemble: tighten clamps firmly (not gorilla-tight — you'll crack the battery case or strip the clamp bolt). Apply battery terminal protector spray or a thin coat of dielectric grease to the outside of each connection. 5. Re-test voltage drop to confirm the fix. 6. Forum tip: voltage drop testing is the single most important electrical diagnostic skill for any motorcycle mechanic, and it's the one most riders never learn. A 'bad battery' that's actually a corroded cable connection is the #1 misdiagnosis in motorcycle starting problems. The battery reads 12.6V sitting there — but under the 100+ amp cranking load, every milliohm of connection resistance steals voltage from the starter. A connection that looks 'okay' can easily have enough corrosion to drop 2-3 volts under load. On Kawasaki Vulcan and other cruiser models, the battery is often in a tight compartment where the terminals are hard to inspect — pull the seat and actually look at them. On any Harley-Davidson, the battery terminal bolts are the weak point: they corrode underneath the nut where you can't see it. Remove the nut, clean the bolt threads with a wire brush, and reinstall. On Honda Gold Wings and other touring bikes with accessory wiring, aftermarket fuse blocks and accessory wires daisy-chained onto the battery terminal add connection points that corrode — clean ALL of them, not just the main cable. The battery terminal brush is a $5 tool that should be in every motorcycle toolbox. Use it once a year when you're checking battery water level (conventional batteries) or doing an annual inspection. Five minutes of cleaning prevents the most common no-start condition on any motorcycle. For bikes stored over winter: disconnect the negative cable to prevent parasitic drain AND apply a light coat of dielectric grease to both terminals before storage.
parts_needed: Battery terminal brush/cleaner tool ($5 — purpose-built, two-ended)
Wire brush (for frame ground and large terminal cleaning)
Dielectric grease or battery terminal protector spray
Battery terminal clamp bolts (replace if corroded through — stainless steel upgrades available)
Multimeter (for voltage drop testing)
New battery cables (if originals are corroded internally or the copper strands are green)

title: Tip-over sensor (bank angle sensor) preventing start after a drop or on uneven ground
description: Modern fuel-injected motorcycles (roughly 2000+) include a tip-over sensor (also called a bank angle sensor, lean angle sensor, or crash sensor) that kills the engine and prevents restart if the bike exceeds a preset lean angle — typically 50-65 degrees from vertical. The sensor uses either a mercury switch, a pendulum contact, or a MEMS accelerometer depending on age and manufacturer. When triggered, the sensor opens the fuel pump relay and/or ignition circuit, preventing the engine from starting. The sensor resets either automatically when the bike is returned to upright, or requires a key-cycle reset (key off → wait 5 seconds → key on). After a parking lot tip-over, a slow-speed drop, or even parking on steeply uneven ground, riders are stranded with a bike that turns on, shows normal dash lights, but absolutely refuses to crank or fire. This affects all modern makes: Honda, Yamaha, Kawasaki, Suzuki, and Harley-Davidson.
symptoms: engine cuts out at high lean angle
causes: Sensor triggered by a slow-speed tip-over or parking lot drop and not reset
Sensor triggered by parking on steeply uneven ground (steep camber, hillside)
Sensor mounting bracket loose from vibration — sensor reads incorrect angle at rest
Sensor internal failure (mercury switch degradation, pendulum stuck, MEMS drift)
Wiring harness connector at sensor loose or corroded
Aftermarket exhaust or bodywork relocation moves sensor from its calibrated position
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnostic: after a drop or tip-over, the bike has normal dash lights but no fuel pump prime sound and won't crank (or cranks but won't fire because the fuel pump is disabled). 2. Reset procedure (try this first — works on most bikes): turn the key OFF. Wait 10 seconds. Turn the key ON. Listen for the fuel pump to prime (2-3 second buzz). If it primes, the sensor has reset and the bike should start. Some bikes require the kill switch to be cycled OFF → RUN as well. 3. If the reset doesn't work after 2-3 attempts with the bike fully upright on flat ground: the sensor may be stuck or faulty. 4. Locate the sensor: typically mounted under the seat, on the frame backbone, or under the tank. Honda: usually under the seat near the ECU. Yamaha: under the fuel tank. Kawasaki: under the seat or behind a side cover. Suzuki: under the seat near the battery. Harley: under the seat or behind the electrical panel. 5. Inspect the sensor mounting: verify it's securely bolted and the mounting angle hasn't changed. If the bracket is bent from a crash, the sensor reads an incorrect lean angle at rest. 6. Test the sensor: unplug the 2-3 wire connector. On most bikes, bridging the signal wire to ground (or disconnecting it, depending on design — check model-specific wiring diagram) will bypass the sensor and allow the bike to start. If the bike starts with the sensor bypassed, replace the sensor. 7. Replacement: $40-120 OEM depending on sensor type and make. MEMS-based sensors on newer bikes are more expensive ($80-120) but far more reliable than mercury or pendulum types. 8. Forum tip: the tip-over sensor is the #1 reason for 'my bike won't start after I dropped it in the parking lot' calls to roadside assistance. The fix is usually just a key cycle — but if you don't know the sensor exists, you'll panic and call a tow truck for a bike that needs nothing more than turning the key off and back on. Every new rider should be taught the reset procedure before their first ride. On Suzuki GSX-R models, the sensor is called the 'TO sensor' in the service manual and it's the small black box mounted under the seat — it has a 3-pin connector. If the sensor fails permanently, the DTC codes C28 or C32 will be stored. On Honda CBR models, the bank angle sensor is integrated near the ECU under the rear seat cowl. On Kawasaki ZX models, it's mounted to the frame under the tank — removing the tank is required for access. On Yamaha R-series, the sensor is on the frame backbone under the fuel tank. On Harley-Davidson FI models (2007+), the tip-over sensor is part of the TSSM (Turn Signal Security Module) on some models or a standalone sensor on others — Harley's system sometimes requires the dealer tool to clear the stored fault code after a sensor replacement. For track riders and stunt riders, a tip-over sensor bypass is a common modification — but for street riding, keep the sensor functional as it also kills the engine in a real crash, preventing fuel pump operation while the bike is on its side potentially leaking fuel.
parts_needed: Tip-over sensor / bank angle sensor (OEM — Suzuki 37890-29G00, Honda 35160-MFL-003, or equivalent)
Sensor mounting bracket (if bent from crash)
Wiring connector (if original is corroded)
Model-specific wiring diagram (for bypass testing — wire colors and logic vary by make)
Multimeter (for sensor circuit testing)

title: Neutral switch failure — false neutral detection preventing in-gear starting
description: The neutral switch is a simple contact switch mounted on the transmission case that grounds a wire when the transmission is in neutral, illuminating the neutral indicator light and telling the ECU that the bike is in neutral (which allows starting without pulling the clutch on some models, or modifies the starting interlock logic on others). When the neutral switch fails, it can either fail open (neutral light never comes on, ECU thinks the bike is always in gear) or fail closed (neutral light always on, ECU thinks the bike is always in neutral). A failed-open neutral switch combined with a failed or marginal clutch switch creates a double-fault condition where the bike won't start in neutral OR with the clutch pulled — a maddening diagnostic puzzle. The switch sits in an oil-filled environment and is sealed, but the internal contacts corrode over time or the external connector corrodes from its location low on the engine case where it's exposed to road spray.
symptoms: neutral light stays on in all gears
neutral light won't illuminate in neutral
causes: Internal switch contacts corroded or worn from age and vibration
External connector corroded from road spray exposure — low mounting location on engine case
Switch O-ring degraded — engine oil weeping past the switch and contaminating the connector
Wire chafed against the engine case or frame — intermittent ground fault
Transmission detent worn — bike doesn't fully engage neutral, switch plunger not fully depressed
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnostic: observe the neutral light. In neutral: should be ON. In any gear: should be OFF. If the light behavior is wrong, the switch or its circuit is faulty. 2. Quick test: with the bike in neutral and the neutral light NOT illuminating — disconnect the single wire from the neutral switch on the engine case. Ground that wire to the engine case with a jumper. If the neutral light comes on, the switch is bad. If the light still doesn't come on, the problem is in the wiring or dash bulb. 3. With the switch connector disconnected, measure resistance from the switch terminal to the engine case. In neutral: should read near zero ohms (switch closed to ground through engine case). In gear: should read open (infinite resistance). If the switch reads open in neutral, replace it. 4. Replacement: the neutral switch is typically a single-bolt sensor threaded into the transmission case, located near the shift linkage. IMPORTANT: there will be a small amount of engine oil behind the switch — have a drain pan ready and work quickly. Some bikes require partial oil drainage first. 5. Apply thread sealant (NOT Loctite — use Permatex thread sealant or Teflon tape) to the new switch threads to prevent oil leaks. 6. Torque to specification — these are small-diameter switches that strip easily. Typically 12-18 Nm (9-13 ft-lbs). 7. Refill oil if needed, check for leaks after running the engine for a few minutes. 8. Forum tip: the neutral switch is a $15-30 part that causes disproportionate grief because its failure mode interacts with the clutch safety switch logic. On many bikes (Honda, Kawasaki, Suzuki), the starting interlock works like this: you can start if (neutral switch = closed) OR (clutch switch = closed). If the neutral switch fails open, you can still start by pulling the clutch. But if BOTH switches are marginal — the neutral switch corroded and the clutch switch partially corroded — you get intermittent no-start that defies diagnosis. Always test both switches together. On Honda VTX, Shadow, and CB models, the neutral switch is on the left side of the engine case near the shift shaft — easily accessible. On Kawasaki Vulcan and ZX models, it's on the top or front of the transmission case. On Suzuki V-Strom and SV models, it's on the left side near the sprocket cover. The switch O-ring is the sneaky failure: it degrades, oil weeps out and runs down the wire to the connector, and the oil-contaminated connector develops a high-resistance connection that works intermittently. If you find oil at the connector, replace the switch AND clean the connector pins. OEM switches always include a new O-ring. Aftermarket switches sometimes don't — verify before installing.
parts_needed: Neutral switch (OEM — Honda 35600-MBA-611, Kawasaki 27010-1461, or equivalent)
Crush washer or O-ring (usually included with OEM switch, verify with aftermarket)
Thread sealant (Permatex or Teflon tape — NOT Loctite)
Small drain pan (for oil that drains when switch is removed)
Engine oil (to top off after switch replacement — typically 50-100ml lost)
Multimeter (for switch continuity testing)

title: Compression release and high-compression starting issues — big singles and twins
description: Large-displacement single-cylinder and twin-cylinder engines (Harley-Davidson Big Twins, Honda XR650, Suzuki DR650, Kawasaki KLR650, Royal Enfield, Yamaha XT/TT series) have high compression ratios that make them extremely difficult for the starter motor to turn over. These engines use automatic compression release mechanisms — typically a centrifugal decompression lever on the camshaft that holds one exhaust valve slightly open at cranking speed, reducing compression until the engine fires and speeds up past the release threshold. When the compression release mechanism fails, the starter motor can't turn the engine over — it either stalls against compression, clicks the starter relay (because the current draw exceeds the relay's capacity), or cranks agonizingly slowly even with a fully charged battery. This is particularly common on Harley-Davidson Twin Cam and M8 engines (both cylinders fire near simultaneously, creating massive compression peaks), Honda XR/CRF650 singles, and Suzuki DR650 singles.
causes: Automatic compression release arm on camshaft worn or broken — no longer lifts exhaust valve at low RPM
Compression release spring broken or weakened — centrifugal mechanism doesn't deploy at cranking speed
High-performance camshaft installed without compression release feature — requires manual decompression or upgraded starter
Carbon buildup on valves increasing effective compression ratio beyond starter motor capability
Incorrect valve lash (too tight) — valves not seating fully, creating inconsistent compression
Weak battery unable to provide the 150-200+ amp surge needed for high-compression engines
fix_procedure: 1. First: verify the battery is fully charged (12.6V+ at rest) and the cables are clean. High-compression engines are the least forgiving of marginal batteries or corroded connections. A battery that starts a 600cc sport bike fine may not have enough cranking amps for a 1700cc V-twin. 2. Compression test: remove both spark plugs (or the single plug on a single-cylinder) and crank the engine. If it spins freely and quickly with the plugs out — the starter motor and battery are fine, and the problem is excessive compression or a failed compression release. If it still won't spin freely with plugs removed — the problem is the starter motor, battery, or cables. 3. Check compression: with a compression gauge threaded into each spark plug hole, crank the engine. Compare readings to specification. On Harley Twin Cam 96/103: expect 150-170 PSI stock. On Twin Cam 110: expect 170-190 PSI. On Honda XR650L: expect 140-160 PSI. Readings significantly above spec suggest carbon buildup (requires top-end cleaning) or incorrect head gasket thickness. 4. Inspect compression release: this requires camshaft access (valve cover removal at minimum). On Harley Twin Cam: the compression release is a small lever on each cam lobe. Verify the lever pivots freely and the spring returns it. On singles (DR650, KLR650, XR650): the decompressor is usually on the exhaust cam lobe. 5. If compression release is broken: replace the camshaft or decompressor mechanism. On Harley Twin Cam engines, aftermarket cams from S&S, Andrews, and Wood Performance are available with or without compression release — ALWAYS specify with compression release for electric-start bikes. 6. Workaround for high-compression engines: use the 'throttle blip' technique — open the throttle slightly (about 1/4) while cranking to reduce the pumping compression. This helps the starter motor on the first revolution. NOT applicable to throttle-by-wire bikes where the ECU controls throttle opening during cranking. 7. Upgraded starter motor: for engines with aftermarket high-compression pistons or cams, the stock starter may simply be inadequate. High-torque replacement starters are available: Compu-Fire for Harley ($200-350), Rick's Motorsport Electrics for Japanese bikes ($150-250). 8. Forum tip: compression-related starting problems are the bane of every Harley owner who's installed a performance cam or high-compression pistons without upgrading the starter motor and battery. The math is simple: if you raise compression from 9.5:1 to 10.5:1 (a common 'bolt-on' cam swap), you increase the torque required to turn the engine over by 15-20%. The stock 1.2kW starter that worked fine at stock compression now struggles. The fix chain: first, verify the automatic compression release is working — if the previous owner installed aftermarket cams without compression release, that's the problem right there. Second, upgrade to a 1.4kW starter (Harley sells one as an OEM upgrade). Third, use the largest battery that physically fits — CCA (Cold Cranking Amps) matters more than Ah capacity for starting. On Harley M8 engines (2017+), the compression release is integrated into the cam design and rarely fails — but the M8's high compression ratio (10.5:1 stock) means the starter works hard from the factory. On Honda XR650L and CRF650L, the auto-decompressor on the exhaust cam is a wear item — check it every valve adjustment interval (every 16,000 miles per Honda). On Suzuki DR650, the decompressor lever on the exhaust cam is robust but the spring that returns it weakens over time — a $8 spring replacement prevents a $400 tow. On Kawasaki KLR650 (Gen 1 and Gen 2), the compression is relatively modest (9.5:1) and starting problems are more often battery or cable related than compression release related. The universal truth for big singles and twins: if the engine cranks slowly when hot (when metals have expanded and compression is at its highest), the compression release or starter capacity is the issue. If it cranks slowly only when cold, the battery or cable connections are the issue.
parts_needed: Compression release mechanism or camshaft with compression release (make/model specific)
Decompressor return spring (for single-cylinder engines with serviceable springs)
High-torque starter motor (Compu-Fire, Rick's Motorsport Electrics — if stock starter is inadequate)
High-CCA battery (CCA rating matters more than Ah for cranking)
Compression gauge with adapter hose (for diagnostic testing)
Valve cover gasket (required for compression release inspection)
